content:
# Joshua Chapter 15 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 238-240
**Source Image:** `JOS_015_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_015_page_002.jpg`, `JOS_015_page_003.jpg`

---

**15:1** And the lot for the tribe of the sons of Judah, according to their clans, was to the border of Edom, the wilderness of Zin southward, at the extreme south.

**15:2** And their southern border was from the end of the Salt Sea, from the tongue that faces southward.

**15:3** And it went out southward of the Ascent of Akrabbim, and passed over to Zin, and went up on the south side of Kadesh-Barnea, and passed over to Hezron, and went up to Addar, and turned about to Karka.

**15:4** And it passed over to Azmon, and went out to the Wadi of Egypt, and the goings out of the border were at the sea. This shall be your southern border.

**15:5** And the eastern border was the Salt Sea as far as the end of the Jordan. And the border on the northern side was from the tongue of the sea, from the end of the Jordan.

**15:6** And the border went up to Beth-Hoglah, and passed over north of Beth-Arabah, and the border went up to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.

**15:7** And the border went up to Debir from the Valley of Achor, and northward, turning toward the Gilgal, which is opposite the Ascent of Adummim, which is south of the wadi. And the border passed over to the waters of En-Shemesh, and its goings out were at En-Rogel.

**15:8** And the border went up by the Valley of Ben-Hinnom to the side of the Jebusite from the south — that is, Jerusalem. And the border went up to the top of the mountain which is before the Valley of Hinnom westward, which is at the end of the Valley of Rephaim northward.

**15:9** And the border marked out from the top of the mountain to the spring of the waters of Nephtoah, and went out to the cities of Mount Ephron. And the border marked out to Baalah — that is, Kiriath-Jearim.

**15:10** And the border turned about from Baalah westward to Mount Seir, and passed over to the side of Mount Jearim on the north — that is, Chesalon — and went down to Beth-Shemesh, and passed over to Timnah.

**15:11** And the border went out to the side of Ekron northward, and the border marked out to Shikkeron, and passed over to Mount Baalah, and went out to Jabneel. And the goings out of the border were at the sea.

**15:12** And the western border was the Great Sea and its coastline. This is the border of the sons of Judah all around, according to their clans.

**15:13** And to Caleb son of Jephunneh he gave a portion in the midst of the sons of Judah, according to the command of YHWH to Joshua: Kiriath-Arba — the father of the Anak — that is, Hebron.

**15:14** And Caleb drove out from there the three sons of the Anak: Sheshai and Ahiman and Talmai, the descendants of the Anak.

**15:15** And he went up from there against the inhabitants of Debir. And the name of Debir formerly was Kiriath-Sepher.

**15:16** And Caleb said, "Whoever strikes Kiriath-Sepher and captures it, to him I will give Achsah my daughter as a wife."

**15:17** And Othniel son of Kenaz, the brother of Caleb, captured it; and he gave to him Achsah his daughter as a wife.

**15:18** And it was, when she came, that she urged him to ask her father for a field. And she got down from the donkey, and Caleb said to her, "What do you want?"

**15:19** And she said, "Give me a blessing, for you have given me the land of the Negev; now give me also springs of water." And he gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

**15:20** This is the inheritance of the tribe of the sons of Judah according to their clans.

**15:21** And the cities at the extreme south of the tribe of the sons of Judah, toward the border of Edom in the Negev, were: Kabzeel and Eder and Jagur,

**15:22** and Kinah and Dimonah and Adadah,

**15:23** and Kedesh and Hazor and Ithnan,

**15:24** Ziph and Telem and Bealoth,

**15:25** and Hazor-Hadattah and Kerioth-Hezron — that is, Hazor —

**15:26** Amam and Shema and Moladah,

**15:27** and Hazar-Gaddah and Heshmon and Beth-Pelet,

**15:28** and Hazar-Shual and Beer-Sheba and Biziothiah,

**15:29** Baalah and Iim and Ezem,

**15:30** and Eltolad and Chesil and Hormah,

**15:31** and Ziklag and Madmannah and Sansannah,

**15:32** and Lebaoth and Shilhim and Ain and Rimmon — all the cities were twenty-nine, with their villages.

**15:33** In the Shephelah: Eshtaol and Zorah and Ashnah,

**15:34** and Zanoah and En-Gannim, Tappuah and Enam,

**15:35** Jarmuth and Adullam, Socoh and Azekah,

**15:36** and Shaaraim and Adithaim and Gederah and Gederothaim — fourteen cities with their villages.

**15:37** Zenan and Hadashah and Migdal-Gad,

**15:38** and Dilean and Mizpeh and Joktheel,

**15:39** Lachish and Bozkath and Eglon,

**15:40** and Cabbon and Lahmas and Chithlish,

**15:41** and Gederoth, Beth-Dagon and Naamah and Makkedah — sixteen cities with their villages.

**15:42** Libnah and Ether and Ashan,

**15:43** and Iphtah and Ashnah and Nezib,

**15:44** and Keilah and Achzib and Mareshah — nine cities with their villages.

**15:45** Ekron, with its towns and its villages.

**15:46** From Ekron westward, all that were beside Ashdod, with their villages.

**15:47** Ashdod, its towns and its villages; Gaza, its towns and its villages — as far as the Wadi of Egypt, and the Great Sea with its coastline.

**15:48** And in the hill country: Shamir and Jattir and Socoh,

**15:49** and Dannah and Kiriath-Sannah — that is, Debir —

**15:50** and Anab and Eshtemoh and Anim,

**15:51** and Goshen and Holon and Giloh — eleven cities with their villages.

**15:52** Arab and Dumah and Eshan,

**15:53** and Janim and Beth-Tappuah and Aphekah,

**15:54** and Humtah and Kiriath-Arba — that is, Hebron — and Zior — nine cities with their villages.

**15:55** Maon, Carmel and Ziph and Juttah,

**15:56** and Jezreel and Jokdeam and Zanoah,

**15:57** Kain, Gibeah and Timnah — ten cities with their villages.

**15:58** Halhul, Beth-Zur and Gedor,

**15:59** and Maarath and Beth-Anoth and Eltekon — six cities with their villages.

**15:60** Kiriath-Baal — that is, Kiriath-Jearim — and Rabbah — two cities with their villages.

**15:61** In the wilderness: Beth-Arabah, Middin and Secacah,

**15:62** and Nibshan and the City of Salt and En-Gedi — six cities with their villages.

**15:63** And the Jebusites, the inhabitants of Jerusalem — the sons of Judah were not able to drive them out. And the Jebusites have dwelt with the sons of Judah in Jerusalem to this day.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Method
Each verse was compared across all three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) and checked against the manuscript images of Leningrad Codex pages 238-240. Where all three translators agreed on a reading, that agreement was adopted. Where they diverged, the manuscript was consulted and the wisest synthesis was chosen, favoring accuracy to the Hebrew while maintaining readable English. This chapter is predominantly geographic boundary descriptions and city lists, where the primary challenges are place-name transliteration and formulaic boundary language.

### Agreements
The three translators demonstrated exceptionally strong agreement across Joshua 15. All three preserve:
- **YHWH** (not "the LORD") in v. 13
- **Sons of Judah** (not "Judahites" or "people of Judah") throughout (vv. 1, 12, 13, 20, 21, 63)
- **Valley of Ben-Hinnom** as the literal patronymic construction in v. 8
- **"Father of the Anak"** as the literal reading of Arba's epithet in v. 13
- The **city count discrepancy** in v. 32 (twenty-nine stated vs. more names listed), preserved without emendation
- The **editorial note** "to this day" in v. 63 as received
- The **"daughters"** idiom for satellite towns (vv. 45-47), though with variation in rendering
- **Shephelah** as a geographic term in v. 33 (with PNEUMA diverging to "foothills")
- The **fourfold geographic structure** of the city lists: Negev, Shephelah, hill country, wilderness

### Major Decisions

**1. Proper name transliteration system (throughout)**
EMET uses maximally Hebrew transliterations (Yehudah, Yerushalayim, Yarden, Chevron, Re'uven, Qiryat-Arba, etc.), while LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard anglicized scholarly forms (Judah, Jerusalem, Jordan, Hebron, Reuben, Kiriath-Arba, etc.). SOPHIA adopts the anglicized forms, consistent with the established SOPHIA convention (see Joshua 4 decisions). These place names have universally recognized English equivalents; full Hebrew transliteration in a geographic catalog of this density would impede readability without adding meaningful precision. See Disagreement Log.

**2. "The tongue" vs. "the bay" for ha-lashon (vv. 2, 5)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve "the tongue" as a literal rendering of the Hebrew geographic metaphor for a bay or inlet of the Dead Sea. PNEUMA renders "the bay." SOPHIA adopts "the tongue" (EMET, LOGOS). The metaphor is comprehensible in English geographic discourse, and preserving it maintains the Hebrew image without obscuring meaning. See Disagreement Log.

**3. "Goings out" vs. "ended at" for totze'ot (vv. 4, 7, 11)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew formulaic term "goings out" for where a boundary terminates. PNEUMA renders more dynamically as "ended at" or "came out at." SOPHIA adopts "goings out" (EMET, LOGOS). This is a technical boundary-survey formula recurring throughout Joshua 13-21, and preserving it maintains the formulaic character of the text. See Disagreement Log.

**4. "Marked out" vs. "extended" vs. "curved" for ta'ar (v. 9)**
EMET renders "marked out," LOGOS renders "extended," PNEUMA renders "curved." The Hebrew ta'ar means "to outline, delineate, trace a boundary." SOPHIA adopts "marked out" (EMET), which most accurately conveys the surveying action of the verb, distinguishing it from the standard movement verbs (went up, went out, passed over) used elsewhere in the boundary description. See Disagreement Log.

**5. "Side" vs. "slope" for kateph (vv. 8, 10, 11)**
EMET renders kateph as "side," LOGOS and PNEUMA render "slope" or "southern slope." The Hebrew kateph literally means "shoulder" and is used for the flank or slope of a hill. SOPHIA adopts "side" (EMET) for vv. 10-11 where the terrain feature is a general flank, but notes that "slope" (LOGOS) is equally defensible. The literal "shoulder" would be too opaque. See Disagreement Log.

**6. "Shephelah" vs. "foothills" (v. 33)**
EMET and LOGOS retain "Shephelah" as a recognized geographic term. PNEUMA translates it as "foothills." SOPHIA adopts "Shephelah" (EMET, LOGOS). This is a technical geographic designation for the specific low hill country between the Judean highlands and the coastal plain, and it is used untranslated in scholarly and archaeological contexts. A rendering as "foothills" loses the specificity of the term. See Disagreement Log.

**7. "Daughters" vs. "towns" for benoteyha (vv. 45-47)**
EMET preserves "her daughters" literally. LOGOS renders "its towns." PNEUMA renders "its surrounding towns." SOPHIA adopts "its towns" (LOGOS) in this context. While "daughters" is the literal Hebrew, the idiom for satellite settlements is so thoroughly conventional that preserving it here — in a geographic catalog — would distract rather than illuminate. The literal sense is recorded in the notes. See Disagreement Log.

**8. "The end of the Jordan" vs. "the mouth of the Jordan" (v. 5)**
EMET renders "the end of the Yarden," LOGOS renders "the mouth of the Jordan," PNEUMA renders "the mouth of the Jordan" / "where the Jordan empties." The Hebrew qetzeh ha-Yarden means "the end/extremity of the Jordan." SOPHIA adopts "the end of the Jordan" (EMET), as qetzeh straightforwardly means "end, extremity." The rendering "mouth" is an interpretive paraphrase — correct geographically but not what the Hebrew says. See Disagreement Log.

**9. "Command of YHWH" vs. "mouth of YHWH" (v. 13)**
EMET renders "the mouth of YHWH" (al-pi YHWH). LOGOS renders "the command of YHWH." PNEUMA renders "as YHWH had directed." The Hebrew pi means literally "mouth" but in the idiom al-pi it means "according to the command/word of." SOPHIA adopts "the command of YHWH" (LOGOS), as this is an established Hebrew idiom where "mouth" signifies authoritative utterance, and "command" captures the idiomatic sense without obscuring it. See Disagreement Log.

**10. "Got down" vs. "dismounted" for va-titznach (v. 18)**
EMET renders "got down from upon the donkey." LOGOS renders "dismounted from the donkey." PNEUMA renders "got down from her donkey." The Hebrew tznach means "to alight, spring down." SOPHIA adopts "got down from the donkey" — a compromise preserving the concrete action (EMET, PNEUMA) while smoothing the syntax. The verb is more vivid than "dismounted," suggesting a quick, purposeful descent. See Disagreement Log.

**11. "Struck / strikes" vs. "attacks" for makkeh (v. 16)**
EMET and LOGOS render "strikes" (Hiphil of nakah). PNEUMA renders "attacks." SOPHIA adopts "strikes" (EMET, LOGOS), preserving the martial directness of the Hebrew verb. See Disagreement Log.

**12. City of Salt vs. Ir-ha-Melach (v. 62)**
EMET transliterates "Ir-ha-Melach." LOGOS and PNEUMA translate as "the City of Salt." SOPHIA adopts "the City of Salt" (LOGOS, PNEUMA). Unlike most place names in this chapter, this one carries transparent meaning in Hebrew, and English translations universally render it rather than transliterate. The meaning — a settlement in the salt wilderness near the Dead Sea — is geographically evocative. See Disagreement Log.

**13. Ha-Rabbah vs. Rabbah (v. 60)**
EMET preserves the definite article "ha-Rabbah." LOGOS and PNEUMA render simply "Rabbah." The LC text has הָרַבָּה with the article. SOPHIA adopts "Rabbah" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) without the article, following standard English convention for this place name, though the article is noted. See Disagreement Log.

**14. Conjunction style in city lists (vv. 21-62)**
EMET preserves the Hebrew conjunction "and" before nearly every city name. LOGOS preserves most conjunctions. PNEUMA drops conjunctions entirely in the city lists, using simple comma separation. SOPHIA preserves the Hebrew conjunction pattern (EMET, LOGOS), maintaining "and" where the Hebrew waw appears. The conjunction-heavy style is characteristic of Hebrew list-making and contributes to the catalog rhythm of this chapter. See Disagreement Log.

**15. "And it was, when she came" vs. "When she arrived" (v. 18)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the wayehi construction ("And it was, when she came"). PNEUMA drops it ("When she arrived"). SOPHIA preserves "And it was, when she came" (EMET, LOGOS), consistent with the SOPHIA convention of preserving wayehi as a structural marker of Hebrew narrative. See Disagreement Log.

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex)
The three manuscript pages (LC folios 238-240) cover Joshua 15 in its entirety. The text is clearly legible in three columns per page with Masoretic vowel pointing and cantillation marks. Key observations:
- The city lists are written continuously in the manuscript without the modern verse breaks that might suggest natural groupings. The regional headers (Negev, Shephelah, hill country, wilderness) are embedded within the text flow.
- The Masorah parva contains marginal annotations on several place names, consistent with the scribal tradition of marking unusual or infrequent forms.
- The text of v. 63 (the Jebusite notice) appears at the end of the chapter as a distinct editorial note, its formulaic "to this day" marking it as a synchronistic observation from the period of composition.
- No significant textual variants are visible between the LC text and what all three translators have rendered. The chapter is textually stable.

---
